title: Macaroni pie
subtitle: Trinidadian baked macaroni in a custard base
excerpt: Short-cut macaroni baked in a rich egg and evaporated milk custard with cheddar, scotch bonnet, spring onion, and black pepper, then cut into squares when cold. Trinidadian macaroni pie is denser and more set than American mac and cheese — it holds its shape when sliced and is eaten as a starchy side rather than a main course. The custard sets firm, the top browns, and the scotch bonnet runs through the whole dish.
sourceNotes: Macaroni pie is a staple of the Trinidadian Sunday lunch table, served alongside stew chicken, rice and peas, and plantain. Unlike American macaroni and cheese, the Trinidadian version uses evaporated milk and eggs to set the pie firmly, making it sliceable. It appears at every Sunday table and at the cricket ground in the same wax-paper wrapped square that it has for generations.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Better made the day before. The pie sets firmer overnight in the fridge and slices cleanly.

content.text: The macaroni must be cooked to just-done before baking — not fully soft, as it will continue cooking in the oven. Undercooked macaroni makes a pie with a stodgy bite; overcooked macaroni makes a pie that collapses. Cook to about 2 minutes short of the packet instruction.

The pie must rest before slicing. Hot from the oven the custard is still liquid at the centre; after 15 minutes' rest and a further 15 minutes of cooling on the worktop, it will be fully set. For the cleanest slices, allow it to cool completely and refrigerate overnight.

Cook the {{pasta-dried}} in well-salted water for 2 minutes less than the packet instruction. Drain well. Whisk the {{eggs}} with the {{evaporated-milk}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{black-pepper}}. Add 100 g of the {{cheddar}}, the {{spring-onion}}, and {{scotch-bonnet}}. Add the drained macaroni and stir to combine. Heat the oven to 180°C. Butter a baking dish (about 25 x 20 cm). Pour in the macaroni mixture and press it down evenly. Scatter the remaining 50 g of cheddar over the top. Bake for 40 to 45 minutes until the top is golden and the custard has set. Rest for 15 minutes before cutting.

items.symptom: Pie runny in the centre when cut
items.cause: Not baked long enough or cut too hot
items.fix: The pie must be fully rested before cutting. If liquid runs out when cutting, return to the oven for 10 more minutes and rest again

items.cause: Too many eggs or baked at too high a temperature
items.fix: Two eggs for 300 g of pasta is the correct ratio. A custard baked too hot becomes rubbery — 180°C is the maximum

items.symptom: Top burns before centre is set
items.cause: Oven too hot or dish too small
items.fix: Cover with foil for the first 25 minutes if the top is browning too fast, then uncover for the final 15 to 20 minutes

content.text: Macaroni pie is one of the fixed points of the Trinidadian Sunday table. Alongside stew chicken, rice and peas (or pelau), plantain, and coleslaw, it appears at every Sunday lunch and at every family occasion. The sliceable, set texture makes it convenient for packed lunches and for the cricket-ground picnics that are a fixture of Trinidadian life. In Britain the Trinidadian diaspora makes macaroni pie as a Sunday staple and for gatherings, a dish that is immediately recognisable as specifically Trinidadian rather than the saucer-style American mac and cheese that most British people know.
